serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of imitation abalone salad as served at a Japanese sushi restaurant is approximately 100 g, presented as a small side dish or appetiser.

nutrient_source.title: Surimi (imitation seafood) and Japanese-style seafood salad composite — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Surimi (NDB 15165), Wakame seaweed (NDB 11667), Cucumber (NDB 11205), Sesame seeds (NDB 12023), Soy sauce (NDB 16124); proportions estimated from standard Japanese restaurant preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for imitation abalone salad as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries using a representative recipe composition: approximately 55% surimi, 20% cucumber, 10% wakame, 8% sesame dressing (sesame oil + soy sauce), 7% sesame seeds. Energy and macros are calculated from weighted component averages. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of surimi and mixed seafood salad — analogous estimate based on surimi GI (~40) and low-GI vegetable components; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for imitation abalone salad. Surimi is a processed fish-starch product; its GI is estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on the International GI Tables (Atkinson et al. 2008) for comparable processed fish-starch products and mixed seafood dishes. Vegetable components (cucumber, wakame) are low-GI. Sesame dressing contributes fat which further attenuates glycemic response. Composite GI estimated at 40 (low). GL per 100 g serving = (40 × 8.5) / 100 = 3.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g) from surimi processing and soy sauce dressing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of imitation abalone salad due to its moderate-to-high sodium content (~480 mg/100 g), primarily from surimi processing additives and soy-based dressing; requesting reduced-sodium soy sauce or consuming a smaller portion (50–60 g) can help keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content (~8.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Imitation abalone salad has a low estimated glycemic index (~40) and a low glycemic load (~3.4 per 100 g serving), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal; the protein from surimi and fat from sesame dressing further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium from surimi and soy sauce; beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from surimi fish base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While imitation abalone salad provides beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids (~1.5 g/100 g) from its surimi fish base and sesame components, its sodium content (~480 mg/100 g) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; portion control and low-sodium soy sauce substitution are recommended to balance cardiovascular benefit against sodium risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Imitation abalone salad (模造アワビサラダ, mozō awabi sarada) is a Japanese-style chilled salad dish commonly served as an appetiser or side dish at Japanese sushi restaurants and casual dining chains such as Sushi Express in Singapore. The dish is built around surimi — a processed fish paste product moulded and flavoured to resemble abalone — combined with thinly sliced cucumber, rehydrated wakame seaweed, and a sesame-based dressing typically containing sesame oil, soy sauce, rice vinegar, and toasted sesame seeds. The texture is a contrast of the tender, slightly chewy surimi pieces against the crisp cucumber and silky wakame. The flavour profile is savoury-umami with a mild nuttiness from sesame and a gentle acidity from the vinegar dressing. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~8.2 g/100 g) from the surimi base, low-to-moderate fat (~3.8 g/100 g) with a favourable polyunsaturated-to-saturated ratio, and a low glycemic index (~40), making it a relatively balanced light dish. Sodium content is moderate-to-high (~480 mg/100 g) due to surimi processing and soy sauce, which is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low GI and low glycemic load make this a suitable option. The dish is not suitable for fish or soy allergen sufferers and is not vegetarian or vegan due to the fish-derived surimi content.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for imitation abalone salad as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries for surimi, wakame, cucumber, and sesame dressing using a representative recipe composition. GI is an analogous estimate based on comparable processed fish-starch products from the International GI Tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the recipe composition is consistent with standard Japanese restaurant preparation.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this composite estimate. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) as surimi and vegetable components do not contain industrially produced trans fats per USDA SR Legacy data. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA SR Legacy component data; a direct laboratory analysis of this dish as served at Sushi Express Singapore would be required for high-confidence values. Sodium value (~480 mg/100 g) reflects the combined contribution of surimi processing additives and soy-based dressing and should be treated as an estimate with ±15% uncertainty. GI set to 40 (low) as an analogous estimate based on processed fish-starch surimi products and low-GI vegetable components per International GI Tables (Atkinson et al. 2008). Food group reclassified from input 'Vegetables' to 'Fish and seafood' as the primary protein and caloric component is surimi (processed fish product).
